data.reasoningText:
The user wants to enable hot reload for their test project so they don't have to set the `DOTNET_WATCH` or `TESTINGPLATFORM_HOTRELOAD_ENABLED` environment variable every time. 

For `dotnet test` with hot reload, the typical approach is to use `dotnet watch test`. But to always enable hot reload, you can configure it in the project file or use a `Directory.Build.props` or a `launchSettings.json`.

Actually, for .NET test projects using the testing platform (`TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport` is true), hot reload during `dotnet test` can be enabled by setting the `TESTINGPLATFORM_HOTRELOAD_ENABLED` environment variable to `1`, or by using `dotnet watch test`.


I'm considering whether there's an MSBuild property equivalent to the environment variable that could be set in the project file itself. The `dotnet watch test` command should handle hot reload automatically, but I need to figure out if there's a way to enable it directly with `dotnet test` without relying on environment variables or the watch wrapper. `dotnet watch test` is the simplest approach since it automatically enables hot reload without any configuration changes, but the user wants this behavior by default. The cleanest solution for making it permanent would be adding a `.runsettings` file with environment variables or setting an MSBuild property in the csproj, though I'm still weighing which approach is most reliable.

Actually, since they're using TestingPlatform with `TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport` already enabled, I can just add the `TestingPlatformHotReloadEnabled` property to their csproj file to make hot reload the default behavior when running `dotnet test`.

data.content: Added `<TestingPlatformHotReloadEnabled>true</TestingPlatformHotReloadEnabled>` to your csproj. Now when you run `dotnet test`, hot reload will be enabled automatically — no environment variable needed. The test runner will watch for file changes and re-run affected tests.
